name: The 'Mantegna Tarocchi' — A Ladder of Being (NOT a Tarot), c. 1465
description:
# Mantegna "Tarocchi" — *not* a tarot

## At a glance

A set of fifty 15th-century engravings, **not a card game and not a tarot deck at all**.
Included in this library precisely to explain the confusion: the name is a later error.
Not by Mantegna, not tarot, not for play or divination — a **didactic image cycle** of
the cosmos and the social order.

## Origin & dating

Two sets survive, the **E-series** (c.1465, the older) and the **S-series** (a little
later). Documentary support includes a Bolognese manuscript dated 1467. Stylistic links
to the Salone dei Mesi at the Palazzo Schifanoia point to artists in the **Ferrarese /
Este** orbit (around Francesco del Cossa); the true engraver is unknown. The attribution
to Andrea Mantegna is spurious.

## Provenance & evidence

Fifty engravings in **five decades** of ten: social ranks (Beggar → Pope), the Muses and
Apollo, the liberal arts, the virtues and cosmic principles, and the celestial spheres.
It is a **ladder of being** — a Renaissance encyclopedia in pictures.

## Game or divination?

**Neither.** No suits, no trick-taking structure, no fortune-telling. Best understood as
**educational prints**, plausibly for instructing upper-class youths — though, honestly,
no document records their actual use. The "Tarocchi" label was pinned on later by writers
who confused them with the picture-rich tarot game.

## The fear question

**Not applicable** — these are humanist study images, openly produced and circulated.
Their inclusion here is corrective: to stop the genealogy absorbing material that merely
*looks* tarot-adjacent.

## Counter-voices

Esoteric authors have repeatedly raided the Mantegna series for "hidden" Neoplatonic or
Hermetic keys to tarot. The series *is* genuinely Neoplatonic — that was mainstream
learned culture — but it is **not an ancestor of the tarot pack**. Treating it as one is
how false genealogies (and the Egyptian story) get built.

## Open questions / corrections owed

- Function (teaching aid? collector's cycle? devotional ladder?) is inferred, not
  documented.
- Relationship, if any, between its iconographic program and early trionfi trumps is a
  matter of influence, not descent.

## Sources & further reading

- "Mantegna Tarocchi," Wikipedia; "Tarocchi di Mantegna, c.1465," World of Playing Cards.
- "The Mantegna Tarocchi and the View of the World in Northern Italy," academia.edu.

sections.Scene: A barefoot, ragged man in torn clothing leans heavily on a tall walking staff in a ruined landscape. His posture is bent, his head bald or close-cropped. Pigs or small animals root around his feet. A coin or token is visible at upper right. The label reads MISERO I.
sections.Symbol: The wretch stands at the base of the entire social-cosmological ladder. His bare feet and broken posture signal total dispossession — no property, no craft, no office. The animals at his feet echo medieval depictions of the lowest estate as closest to brute nature. Numbered I, he is not chaos but the necessary ground of the hierarchy.

sections.Scene: A young man walks briskly in profile, carrying a large spherical vessel or urn balanced on one arm; a trailing cloth drapes over the other. He wears a short courtly tunic and moves with purposeful lightness. The label reads FAMEIO II.
sections.Symbol: The famiglio (household servant or page) is the first rung above the misero — part of a household, already incorporated into civic life. The rounded vessel he carries echoes the sphere motif that will recur throughout the higher series; here it is a domestic object, not a cosmic one. Service is the entry point to social belonging.

sections.Scene: An interior workshop scene: a craftsman bends over a workbench, tools in hand. A forge or fire glows at left; a child assistant stands behind him; shelves with objects line the background. The labor is concentrated and skilled. The label reads ARTIXAN III.
sections.Symbol: The artisan — craftsman or mechanic — embodies productive skill as a social category distinct from trade or nobility. The forge at left signals transformation of raw material, a creative act bounded by guild and guild secrecy. The child helper marks the transmission of craft through apprenticeship rather than blood.

sections.Scene: A merchant in long robes and a hood stands quietly, reading or consulting a folded document or letter. His posture is contemplative, turned slightly inward, absorbed in text. The label reads MERCHADANTE IIII.
sections.Symbol: The merchant reads — an act that marks him as literate and engaged with abstract exchange rather than manual labor. The letter or bill of lading represents the commercial economy: credit, contract, correspondence across distance. He stands between artisan and gentleman, defined by information and mobility rather than land or tool.

sections.Scene: A well-dressed gentleman stands in an outdoor landscape, a falcon perched on his right fist. Behind him, a young page-attendant; at his feet, a hunting dog. The gentleman's posture is easy, aristocratic, commanding. The label reads ZINTILOMO V.
sections.Symbol: The falcon marks noble leisure-as-power: falconry required land, leisure, trained birds, and attendants — all signs of the gentle estate. The dog at his feet reinforces the hunting context. This figure does not labor but commands; the page behind him materializes his social elevation as a human extension of his will.

sections.Scene: A knight in fine courtly dress (not battle armor) stands with composed authority. A young page or squire looks up at him from one side. The composition emphasizes a vertical hierarchy between the two figures. The label reads CHAVALIER VI.
sections.Symbol: The chevalier embodies the intersection of martial function and social rank — the knight as peacetime courtier rather than battlefield combatant. The page who looks up signals the transmission of chivalric culture through service and imitation. Numbered VI, the knight is above the gentleman but below the Doge, suggesting that civic authority outranks martial title.

sections.Scene: A Venetian Doge stands in dignified profile-to-three-quarter pose, wearing full official state robes and the distinctive corno dogale — the horn-shaped ducal cap of Venice. His bearing is formal and self-contained. The label reads DOXE VII.
sections.Symbol: The Doge is the highest elected civic office in Renaissance Italy's most powerful republic. His inclusion here — above knight and below king — maps the Ferrarese engraver's political world: the Serenissima as a peak of republican civic virtue, surpassed only by monarchs and emperors. The corno is an instantly legible sign of Venetian sovereignty.

sections.Scene: A young crowned king sits on a throne, holding a scepter or staff, hair long and flowing, in formal frontal presentation. The pose is hieratic and still. The label reads RE VIII.
sections.Symbol: The king is the highest hereditary secular authority below the emperor — the embodiment of temporal law and dynastic legitimacy. His youth and long hair recall the rex iuvenis of medieval kingship iconography: the king as warrior-ideal rather than worn administrator. Numbered VIII, he is penultimate in secular power.

sections.Scene: An older bearded emperor sits beneath a draped canopy, holding an orb in one hand; an eagle rests at his feet. The composition is grander and more canopied than the king's. The label reads IMPERATOR VIIII.
sections.Symbol: The emperor holds the orbis terrarum — the globe of earthly dominion — while the eagle (Jupiter's bird, also the symbol of the Holy Roman Empire) legitimates his universal claim. The canopy distinguishes him from the mere king: his authority extends over other kings. Numbered IX, he is the apex of secular hierarchy, yielding only to the spiritual.

sections.Scene: A pope sits enthroned in full frontal hieratic pose, wearing the triple tiara (triregnum) and holding the crossed keys of Saint Peter and a papal staff. The posture is maximally formal. The label reads PAPA X.
sections.Symbol: The pope closes the social ladder as the apex of spiritual authority over temporal authority — a claim contested in the Quattrocento but still encoded here. The triple tiara signifies sovereignty over heaven, purgatory, and earth. The keys of Peter are the foundational symbol of papal power. Numbered X, the pope is the summit of the human estates before the ladder ascends into mythological and cosmic registers.

sections.Scene: A barefoot woman plays a long vertical flute or aulos, fingers covering its stops. An ornamental urn or chalice stands at right; a round orb rests at her feet. Rocky landscape and a faint figure behind her. The label reads CALIOPE XI.
sections.Symbol: Calliope is the Muse of epic poetry — the highest of the Muses in classical tradition. Her flute is an unusual but Ferrarese attribute, possibly conflating her with Euterpe; the orb at her feet links her to the celestial music of the spheres. She opens the second series, bridging the human estates and the mythological-cosmic register.

sections.Scene: A woman stands in open landscape, holding a large celestial sphere or globe balanced on her palm and a long pointer or stick in her other hand. She gazes out toward the viewer. The label reads VRANIA XII.
sections.Symbol: Urania is the Muse of astronomy — the only Muse whose domain is a Liberal Art rather than a performance art, anticipating the Astrologia in Series C. The celestial globe she carries links her directly to the Ptolemaic cosmological sequence that culminates in Series A. The pointer signals instruction: she teaches the movements of the heavens.

sections.Symbol: Terpsichore is the Muse of dance and choral song; her lute here conflates her domain with the lyric tradition. sections.Symbol: Erato is the Muse of lyric and erotic poetry, often shown with a cithara; here the frame drum suggests her connection to rhythmic, bodily verse. The drum is also associated with Dionysian ritual, marking love poetry as ecstatic and embodied rather than purely intellectual. A gold-washed orb at her feet echoes the cosmic sphere motif.

sections.Scene: A woman carries a set of pan pipes (syrinx) in flowing robes, striding through a hilly landscape with a distant city. Her posture is confident and forward-moving. The label reads POLIMNIA XV.
sections.Symbol: Polyhymnia is the Muse of sacred hymn and mime. The pan pipes link her to pastoral and religious music (the syrinx was sacred to Pan but also used in religious contexts). Her name means 'she of many hymns,' making her the Muse of praise poetry and liturgical composition — appropriate for a Ferrarese court that blended secular and sacred patronage.

sections.Scene: A woman sits or kneels with face upturned, eyes and expression showing inspiration or song. She holds pan pipes. Two trees stand on either side behind her; a flowing foliate border decorates the lower edge. The label reads TALIA XVI.
sections.Symbol: Thalia is the Muse of comedy and pastoral poetry. Her upturned, inspired face — unusual in this series — conveys the moment of poetic inspiration rather than skilled performance. The foliate border and trees mark her domain as the natural, fertile world of comedy and pastoral, as distinct from the civic world of epic or hymn.

sections.Scene: A woman strides powerfully, blowing a long straight trumpet or horn with full force, her body angled into the effort. Hair streams back; landscape with hills and distant structure behind her. The label reads MELPOMENE XVII.
sections.Symbol: Melpomene is the Muse of tragedy. The trumpet here is an unusual attribute — she is more commonly shown with a tragic mask — but the long horn evokes the theatrical aulos and the proclamatory function of tragedy as public civic event. Her forceful stride mirrors the momentum of tragic narrative.

sections.Symbol: Euterpe is the Muse of music and flute-playing — the Muse most directly associated with instrumental performance. The simple recorder and pastoral setting strip away all social hierarchy; she is pure musical pleasure in a natural setting. Her gold-washed sphere at lower left links even this most lyric figure to the cosmic-numerical series above.

sections.Scene: A woman rides on the back of a large swan or goose in motion, the bird prominent in the lower half of the image. She holds a small book or vessel; a ribbon trails around her. A distant coastal view. The label reads CLIO XVIIII.
sections.Symbol: Clio is the Muse of history. The swan is Apollo's bird, marking her as part of the divine chorus; riding it implies that history is carried forward by divine breath as much as human memory. The small book she holds is the scroll of historical record. She is the last of the nine Muses before Apollo himself.

sections.Scene: Apollo sits enthroned, crowned, holding an olive branch in one hand. Two swans flank him; a star-filled sphere rests at his feet; a sun-disc symbol with radiating spokes appears lower left. The label reads APOLLO XX.
sections.Symbol: Apollo closes the Muses series as their divine source. The olive branch signals Apollonian peace and victory; the swans are his sacred birds, markers of poetry and prophecy. The star-sphere at his feet links him to the celestial order that the next series — Liberal Arts — will systematize. The sun-disc is his astronomical identity as Sol.

sections.Scene: An older woman in a heavy hooded cloak and mantle faces the viewer with a stern or expressive face, holding a long rod or switch (ferule) in one hand and a small ink vessel or water pot in the other. The setting is austere, nearly interior. The label reads GRAMMATICA XXI.
sections.Symbol: Grammar is the first and foundational Liberal Art — the gateway through which all language-based learning passes. The ferule (disciplinary rod) and ink vessel are the schoolteacher's instruments: the rod corrects, the ink records. Grammar's severity of posture distinguishes her from the Muses above: she is discipline, not inspiration.

sections.Scene: A woman in profile, with elaborate curly hair, strides dynamically while holding up a theatrical or debating mask — a face with expressive features. She reaches out with her free hand. The label reads LOICA XXII.
sections.Symbol: Logic holds a mask because logical argument tests the appearance of things against their underlying truth — the mask is what seems, which logic must pierce or expose. The dynamic stride and reaching hand signal the adversarial, dialectical movement of logical disputation. Logic is the second trivium art, transforming Grammar's foundations into structured argument.

sections.Scene: A crowned woman stands regally, holding a long sword in one hand; two small putto figures crouch at her feet, one appearing to blow a trumpet. She wears an ornate jeweled garment. The label reads RHETORICA XXIII.
sections.Symbol: Rhetoric is the queen of the Trivium — and she is literally crowned here. The sword signals persuasion as force; the crown, authority over speech itself. The putti with the trumpet are heralds of rhetoric's proclamatory power. The crown and jeweled dress place her above Grammar and Logic in worldly influence, even if not in epistemic priority.

sections.Scene: A woman seated on clouds above a landscape gestures with a measuring rod toward geometric forms — a circle, a square, and a triangle — floating at her left. She is elevated above the earth. The label reads GEOMETRIA XXIIII.
sections.Symbol: Geometry is the first Quadrivium art — the study of form and space abstracted from matter. Her elevation on clouds signals that geometry operates above the contingent world; the pure forms (circle, square, triangle) she gestures toward are Platonic ideals made visible. The landscape below is the world her forms precede and govern.

sections.Scene: A woman in a modest headscarf stands in a plain, austere frontal pose, her hands joined quietly at her waist, gaze cast downward. A small sphere rests at her feet. The label reads ARITMETRICHA XXV.
sections.Symbol: Arithmetic is the most abstract of the Liberal Arts — pure number, with no visible instrument but the self-sufficient downward gaze of inward calculation. Her plainness contrasts with Geometry's clouds and Rhetoric's crown: number needs no theater. The sphere at her feet is the monad, the numerical unit from which all quantities arise.

sections.Scene: A woman seated on a swan plays a recorder or fipple flute; surrounding her on the ground is an array of instruments: a lute, a portable organ, another stringed instrument. The scene is rich with sonic objects. The label reads MVSICHA XXVI.
sections.Symbol: Musica in the Liberal Arts tradition is not performance but the mathematics of sound — ratio, proportion, consonance. The instruments at her feet are the empirical realm; she sits above them on Apollo's swan. The profusion of instruments signals that all musical forms are unified by the underlying numerical ratios she embodies.

sections.Scene: A young man crowned with laurel sits amid flowering plants, playing a small recorder or flute. Behind him, an elaborate ornamental fountain with a tall finial ewer. At his lower right, a starred sphere. The label reads POESIA XXVII.
sections.Symbol: Poetry appears here as an extra-canonical seventh Liberal Art — a Quattrocento humanist insertion that reflects the revival of classical poetics. The laurel crown marks the poetic vates (prophet-poet); the flowering ground is the fertile chaos of inspiration; the fountain is the Castalian spring of the Muses. The starred sphere links poetry to cosmic order.

sections.Scene: A woman holds a long spear or lance in one hand and a rectangular shield or tablet bearing a Medusa-face in the other. Her chest shows scaled armor or an aegis-like breastplate. She is warrior-philosopher in bearing. The label reads PHILOSOFIA XXVIII.
sections.Symbol: Philosophy is armed with the aegis — the Gorgon-bearing shield of Athena, goddess of wisdom. The lance signals that true philosophy is militant, capable of slaying ignorance as Perseus slew Medusa. This Athena-coded figure places philosophy above the seven Liberal Arts as their source and crown, synthesizing them into unified wisdom.

sections.Scene: A winged angel with a star-crown walks in profile, holding a long pointer or measuring rod; beside her, a large sphere filled with star-symbols. The figure moves with quiet purpose. The label reads ASTROLOGIA XXVIIII.
sections.Symbol: Astrology here is not fortune-telling but the medieval-Renaissance discipline of studying celestial influences — the bridge between the mathematical arts (Quadrivium) and the cosmic hierarchy of the spheres above. Her wings mark her as a being who moves between terrestrial and celestial registers. The starred sphere she carries is identical to the cosmic spheres that will appear in Series A.

sections.Scene: A bearded male figure — unusual in this predominantly female series — stands above a large semicircular form filled with stars (the firmament). He wears a laurel crown and appears in armor or formal dress, looking upward. The label reads THEOLOGIA XXX.
sections.Symbol: Theology is the only male figure in the Liberal Arts series, and the only one who gazes upward rather than inward or outward. His masculine form signals that theology, unlike the arts it crowns, is not a discipline to be mastered but a source revealed — the divine science. The star-hemisphere at his feet is the boundary between human knowledge and divine mystery.

sections.Scene: A winged youth walks in profile, holding up a glowing Sun face — a disc with a human countenance and radiating rays — on his outstretched palm. A treeline landscape stretches behind him. The label reads ILIACO XXXI.
sections.Symbol: The Iliaco (solar genius) is a personified cosmic force, one of three genii between the planetary spheres and the virtues. He carries the Sun not as Sol the god (who appears in Series A) but as a cosmological principle — the animating solar intelligence distributed through the hierarchy. The winged form signals his role as cosmic messenger rather than deity.

sections.Scene: A winged male youth walks in profile through a topiary garden, raising a winged dragon or sea-serpent creature in one hand. The label reads CHRONICO XXXII.
sections.Symbol: Chronico is the genius of time — the Dragon or serpent he carries is the emblem of Chronos/Saturn in his role as devourer of moments. The garden setting is ironic: topiary implies controlled nature, yet time is uncontrollable. He is the intermediary between the eternal planetary order and temporal human experience.

sections.Scene: A winged male youth stands frontally, holding a large star-filled celestial sphere on his palm. Trees frame a landscape behind him. The label reads COSMICO XXXIII.
sections.Symbol: Cosmico is the genius of the cosmic order — he holds the starry sphere that embodies the Ptolemaic universe as a unified whole. Unlike Astrologia (who points at the sphere) or Ottava Spera (who contemplates it), Cosmico holds the cosmos in his hand as an intelligible object. He is the tutelary spirit of the entire cosmological system.

sections.Scene: A woman pours liquid between two vessels — one a small amphora she tilts, the other she pours into at a lower angle. She looks downward with calm concentration. A wheel or circular object lies at her feet. The label reads TEMPERANCIA XXXIIII.
sections.Symbol: Temperance is depicted in her classical form: the mixing of water and wine, the dilution of excess. The wheel at her feet may reference Fortuna's wheel tamed by moderation, or it may be a millstone — a symbol of measured, continuous motion. She is the first of the four Cardinal Virtues in this series.

sections.Scene: A two-faced figure: a young woman's face looks forward while an older bearded man's face appears on the back of the same head. She holds a mirror on a candlestick with a small winged putto at its top. A dragon crouches at her feet. The label reads PRVDENCIA XXXV.
sections.Symbol: Prudence's two faces are past (the elder, bearded) and present (the young woman) — she sees backward to learn and forward to act. The mirror reflects truth without distortion; the winged putto suggests angelic counsel. The dragon at her feet is conquered appetite, subdued by foresight. She is the Virtue most associated with governance and wisdom.

sections.Scene: A crowned woman kneels beside a broken column, gripping it with one hand and holding a mace or club in the other. A subdued lion crouches at her feet. The label reads FORTEZA XXXVI.
sections.Symbol: Fortitude combines the broken column (physical endurance, the Samsonian body) with the lion (courage mastered). The crown signals that Strength is sovereign, not brutal — this is the virtue that enables all other virtues by holding firm under pressure. The mace is the instrument of deterrence; the lion at her feet is not killed but tamed.

sections.Scene: A woman stands holding a large upright sword in one hand and balanced scales in the other. A crane or stork stands at her feet. The label reads IVSTICIA XXXVII.
sections.Symbol: Justice holds the two classical instruments of adjudication: the sword (enforcement) and scales (measurement). The crane at her feet carries the classical attribute of vigilance — the crane sleeps with a stone in one claw so that if it dozes, the stone falls and wakes it. Justice, like the crane, must never sleep.

sections.Scene: A woman holds up a cup or vessel from which small spheres or coins pour downward. Below her, a pelican feeds its young from its own breast. The label reads CHARITA XXXVIII.
sections.Symbol: Charity's pouring hand gives materially; the pelican at her feet gives vitally — the pelican pierces its own breast to feed its young, the supreme image of self-sacrifice in Christian iconography. Together they define Charity as both material generosity and total self-giving. She is the highest of the three Theological Virtues, completing the virtues series.

sections.Scene: A young woman stands with hands clasped in prayer, face and eyes lifted toward a radiant burst of light or star in the upper corner of the image. At her feet, a phoenix or eagle rising from flames or a nest. The label reads SPERANZA XXXVIIII.
sections.Symbol: Hope's gaze is entirely upward — toward the divine light that grounds all eschatological expectation. The phoenix at her feet is the supreme emblem of resurrection and renewal: it burns to rise again, as hope persists through destruction. She does not grasp or perform; she waits, in the active posture of faith-directed anticipation.

sections.Symbol: Faith (Fede) holds the Eucharist — the sacramental body of Christ — as the object of her belief, not an abstract proposition. She closes the virtue series with explicit sacramental imagery.

sections.Symbol: Luna drives her chariot along the lowest sphere of the Ptolemaic cosmos — the sphere of the Moon, closest to earth and most changeable. The crescent she holds is her attribute and her body; the ship below is subject to tidal forces she governs. The chariot form identifies this as a planetary deity in the Roman triumphal-procession tradition.

sections.Symbol: Mercury carries all his classical attributes: the caduceus (herald's staff, emblem of commerce and communication), the winged cap (speed of thought), the rooster (dawn, commerce), and the head of Argus (killed by Hermes with his own cunning). As the sphere closest to the Moon, Mercury governs wit, trade, and exchange.

sections.Symbol: Venus is the only planetary sphere depicted as a mythological scene rather than a single figure — the Birth of Venus or Triumph of Venus, populated with Cupid and Graces. She is the third sphere, governing desire as a structuring force.

sections.Symbol: The Sun's chariot carries the mythological warning of Phaeton — the mortal who drove the solar chariot and fell. The scorpion above marks the zodiacal sign at which Phaeton lost control (a Virgilian detail). As the fourth and central sphere, the Sun is the pivot of the Ptolemaic cosmos: the middle planet in a seven-planet system.

sections.Symbol: Mars is enthroned in an architectural niche, making him a cult image as much as a deity — this is Mars as patron of armies rather than active warrior. The wolf at his feet is his sacred animal (the she-wolf of Rome, also the animal of Romulus and Remus). The wheeled platform anticipates his role in the triumphal procession tradition.

sections.Scene: Jupiter sits within a vesica piscis or pointed oval mandorla, holding a scepter; an eagle perches at the top of the mandorla. At his feet, small defeated or prostrate figures on the ground. The label reads IVPITER XXXXVI.
sections.Symbol: Jupiter's mandorla — the form used in Christian art for Christ in Majesty — explicitly maps Roman theological authority onto the king of the gods. The eagle above is his attribute and the emblem of imperial power. The prostrate figures at his feet are subdued human forces. As the sixth sphere, Jupiter is the planet of justice, law, and kingship.

sections.Symbol: Saturn is the outermost and slowest of the seven classical planets, associated with old age, melancholy, and the devouring of time. The children at his feet invoke the myth of Saturn eating his offspring — the planet that destroys what it produces. The ram-horn identifies him with Kronos/Chronos and the agricultural deity of the Golden Age, now aged and devouring.

sections.Symbol: The eighth sphere is the sphere of fixed stars — the boundary of the measurable cosmos in Ptolemaic astronomy. The angel who holds it is the motive intelligence of this sphere, a being of pure contemplation; gazing upward, she looks toward the Primum Mobile and Prima Causa that lie beyond. The dense stars on the sphere represent all constellations simultaneously.

sections.Scene: A large winged angel moves in a dramatically swirling, windswept pose — robes billowing, body tilted — pushing or spinning a large golden sphere. The dynamism of the image contrasts with all others in the series. The label reads PRIMO MOBILE XXXXVIIII.
sections.Symbol: The Primum Mobile (First Mover) is the ninth sphere in the expanded Ptolemaic model — a transparent sphere whose motion drives all the spheres below it. The dramatic, swirling angel embodies ceaseless, unceasing rotation: this sphere never stops, never fluctuates. Its motion is the clock of the cosmos. The golden sphere — the only one rendered in gold — is luminous with transmitted divine impulse.

sections.Scene: A pure geometric diagram fills the entire image: concentric circles radiate outward from a central point, with alternating rings of gold and line-engraving, suggesting the nested spheres of the Ptolemaic universe. Text appears at the top (partially inverted or upside down). No human or divine figure is depicted. The label reads PRIMA CAUSA L.
sections.Symbol: The Prima Causa (First Cause) is God as Unmoved Mover — the Aristotelian principle beyond the spheres, outside the cosmos, that initiates all motion without itself moving. The absence of any figure is the theological point: the First Cause is not a being among beings but the ground of being itself. The concentric-circle diagram is the Ptolemaic cosmos seen from outside, a map of the universe as a closed, ordered system flowing from a single divine source.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.About this series: The ten conditions of humankind, from the Beggar to the Pope — the social ladder that begins the ascent.
sections.Scene: A composite view of the ten social estates — from barefoot misero through artisan, merchant, gentleman, knight, Doge, king, emperor, and pope — as a unified ladder of human society. The Luna chariot image is used as representative plate.
sections.Symbol: The five-fold social series (E) encodes a complete Renaissance theory of social order: poverty at the base, spiritual sovereignty at the apex, with craft, commerce, nobility, civic office, and temporal monarchy as ascending rungs. Together the estates map the entire human world before the ladder ascends into mythological and cosmic registers.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.About this series: Apollo and the nine Muses — the inspired arts that lift the soul above mere estate.
sections.Scene: Apollo and his nine Muses — Calliope, Urania, Terpsichore, Erato, Polyhymnia, Thalia, Melpomene, Euterpe, Clio — appear together as the divine source of all arts and learning. Apollo is enthroned with swans and a cosmic sphere; the Muses each carry instruments of their domain.
sections.Symbol: The Muses series positions classical mythology as the necessary intermediary between human social life and systematic knowledge. Apollo's patronage of all nine arts — from epic poetry to history and astronomy — establishes the classical gods as personifications of cognitive and creative faculties rather than mere narrative figures.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.About this series: Grammar to Theology — the trivium, quadrivium, and the higher sciences, the disciplines of the mind.
sections.Scene: The seven Liberal Arts — Grammar, Logic, Rhetoric, Geometry, Arithmetic, Music, and Poetry — plus Philosophy, Astrology, and Theology form a complete encyclopedic programme. Grammar holds her rod and vessel; the series ascends from discipline to cosmic contemplation.
sections.Symbol: The Liberal Arts series is the educational core of the Mantegna Tarocchi: the Trivium (Grammar, Logic, Rhetoric) trains language and argument; the Quadrivium (Geometry, Arithmetic, Music, Astronomy/Astrology) trains number and cosmos. Poetry, Philosophy, and Theology extend the programme beyond the school curriculum into the humanist and theological.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.About this series: The three world-daemons — the living spirits of the Sun, of Time, and of the World.
sections.Scene: Three cosmic genii — Iliaco (solar), Chronico (temporal), and Cosmico (universal) — bridge the Liberal Arts and the Cardinal Virtues, carrying the sun, time-serpent, and star-sphere respectively. Together with the seven Cardinal and Theological Virtues, they form the ethical-cosmological register.
sections.Symbol: The genii are unusual presences in the Mantegna Tarocchi — not gods, not allegories, but semi-personified cosmic forces that mediate between intellectual knowledge and moral virtue. Their series suggests that the cosmos itself is ethically structured: the movement of celestial bodies and the practice of virtue are aspects of a single divine order.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.About this series: The four cardinal and three theological virtues — the moral perfection of the soul.
sections.Scene: The four Cardinal Virtues (Temperance, Prudence, Fortitude, Justice) and three Theological Virtues (Charity, Hope, Faith) appear together with three cosmic genii, forming the ethical register that links human moral life to celestial order. Temperance pours; Prudence shows two faces; Justice holds scales and sword; Faith raises the Eucharist.
sections.Symbol: The virtues series in the Mantegna Tarocchi does not follow the standard order (Prudence, Justice, Fortitude, Temperance) but interweaves Cardinal and Theological Virtues with cosmic genii, suggesting that moral excellence is cosmologically grounded — not merely a human achievement but participation in divine order.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.About this series: The seven planetary spheres rising to the fixed stars, the Prime Mover, and the First Cause — the cosmos itself, ending in God.
sections.Scene: The seven classical planets (Luna, Mercury, Venus, Sol, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn) plus the Eighth Sphere of fixed stars, the Primum Mobile, and the Prima Causa form the full Ptolemaic cosmological sequence. Luna drives a chariot above the earth; Prima Causa appears as a pure geometric diagram.
sections.Symbol: Series A maps the Ptolemaic universe as a nested hierarchy of intelligences and spheres: each planet is a moving deity, each higher sphere a progressively more unified power, culminating in the unmoved Prima Causa. This is not merely astronomy but cosmological theology — the universe as a structured descent of divine being into matter.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.The whole ascent: Read in order, the 50 plates form one continuous Neoplatonic ascent: from the Beggar at the bottom of human society, up through the estates, the inspiring Muses, the disciplines of knowledge, the cosmic genii and the seven virtues, then the planetary spheres, to the fixed stars, the Prime Mover, and finally the First Cause — God. It is a map of the soul's climb from matter to the divine.
sections.Why it is here (and why it is NOT a taro: The 'Mantegna Tarocchi' is the most famous false relative of the tarot: a 15th-century Italian engraving set, often shelved beside tarot and even called 'Tarocchi', but with no suits, no 22 trumps, and no game. (It is also not by Mantegna.) Its imagery — virtues, planets, estates — overlaps the tarot trumps because both drew on the same Renaissance iconographic vocabulary, which is exactly why the two are confused. Including it here lets the genealogy say clearly: this is a cousin in imagery, not an ancestor in fact.
sections.Scene: The complete Mantegna Tarocchi as a unified cosmological ladder: fifty engravings arranged in five series of ten, ascending from human social life through culture and knowledge to cosmic forces and divine source. The entire programme is contained within this single ordered structure.
sections.Symbol: The Scala Naturae (Great Chain of Being) made pictorial: the Tarocchi maps the complete Renaissance universe from bare poverty to the Prima Causa, encoding the Neoplatonic principle that all beings participate in a graded hierarchy of being, from lowest matter to pure spirit. The fifty plates are not illustrations of separate topics but a single continuous ascent.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “arts” (10).
sections.Scene: The Liberal Arts as keyword: Grammar, Logic, Rhetoric, Geometry, Arithmetic, Music, Astronomy, Poetry, Philosophy, and Theology form the complete encyclopedic programme of humanist education, from disciplinary foundations to cosmic and divine knowledge.
sections.Symbol: Systematized knowledge as participation in cosmic order: the Quadrivium arts (number, space, sound, time) are not merely useful skills but mathematical descriptions of the universe's structure. The Liberal Arts series is the Mantegna Tarocchi's most explicit claim that education is cosmological.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “estates” (10).
sections.Scene: The social estates series (I–X) distilled to its governing theme: the complete ordering of human society from abject poverty to supreme spiritual authority, as a ladder of civic, temporal, and ecclesiastical ranks.
sections.Symbol: Social hierarchy as cosmic law: the Renaissance understanding that every human role — from beggar to pope — has its place in a divinely ordered structure. The estates are not accidental but necessary rungs in the ascent toward the universal order the higher series will map.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “muses” (10).
sections.Scene: Apollo and the nine Muses as the keyword for classical cultural authority: the divine source and patronage of all poetic, performative, and intellectual arts, positioned as the mythological foundation for the Liberal Arts above.
sections.Symbol: Inspiration as cosmic force: the Muses are not metaphors but real intelligences in the Neoplatonic framework — intermediaries between divine order and human creative faculty. Apollo's solar identity connects them to the planetary spheres; their number nine prefigures the nine spheres of Series A.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “spheres” (10).
sections.Scene: The planetary spheres as keyword: Luna through Saturn, the Eighth Sphere, Primum Mobile, and Prima Causa form the complete Ptolemaic cosmological hierarchy from the nearest heavenly body to the unmoved divine source.
sections.Symbol: The cosmos as an ordered descent from divine unity into material multiplicity: each sphere is a mode of intelligence, each planet a divine power governing its terrestrial domain. Series A is the Mantegna Tarocchi's cosmological climax — the universe as a complete, self-enclosed, divinely animated system.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “virtues” (7).
sections.Scene: The Cardinal and Theological Virtues as keyword: Temperance, Prudence, Fortitude, Justice, Charity, Hope, and Faith form the complete ethical register that links human moral practice to cosmic and divine order.
sections.Symbol: Virtue as cosmological participation: the virtues in this series are not merely moral qualities but cosmic powers — intelligences embedded in the structure of reality that the virtuous person aligns with. Their placement between the genii and the planetary spheres in the Tarocchi's order is not accidental.

metadata.deck: Master of the E-series · People & Institutions

sections.Description: Cards in this deck sharing the keyword “genii” (3).
sections.Scene: The three cosmic genii — Iliaco (solar intelligence), Chronico (temporal force), Cosmico (universal order) — as keyword for the intermediate register between knowledge and virtue, between intellectual achievement and moral excellence.
sections.Symbol: The genii occupy the pivot of the Mantegna Tarocchi's cosmological ladder: between the human arts and the cosmic virtues, they embody the principle that learning must be animated by cosmic forces to become wisdom. They are personified structural principles rather than gods or allegories.

sections.Description: Cross-cutting facets derived from shared keywords — the same card appears under every keyword it carries.
sections.Scene: The axis of the entire Mantegna Tarocchi system: the vertical spine that connects human social life (Series E), classical culture (Series D), systematic knowledge (Series C), cosmic forces (Series B), and celestial-divine order (Series A) into a single programme of Renaissance humanist cosmology.
sections.Symbol: The cosmological axis mundi: the Tarocchi's five-series structure encodes the Renaissance synthesis of Aristotelian cosmology, Neoplatonic emanation, classical mythology, medieval scholasticism, and Christian theology into a single visual programme. Each series is a register of being; the entire set is a map of reality from immanence to transcendence.
